stories_completed.name: Replace with next/image in 2 photo components, add Supabase remotePatterns to next.config.ts, and add server-side validatePhotoFile call in uploadPhoto server action

factory_decisions.decision: AI Assist implemented as a deterministic keyword-matched suggester (not a live LLM call)
factory_decisions.context: AC-TC-3 specifies 'the AI suggests specific task/material additions or substitutions with accept/reject buttons' and notes 'Requires LLM integration (Claude API or similar)'. The project has no Anthropic/OpenAI SDK in package.json, no API key in .env.local, and no server-side LLM helper.
alternatives_considered: Add @anthropic-ai/sdk + ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + streaming response handling within this story — doubles the story scope, introduces a new secret to manage, and conflates LLM infra work with the UX story
Stub with a random/static suggestion regardless of input — fails AC-TC-3's specificity requirement
Skip AC-TC-3 entirely — leaves the chat panel un-demoable and blocks AC-TC-4
factory_decisions.rationale: suggestFromMessage(message, snapshot) is a pure function with a stable interface — it returns AssistantSuggestion | null. A keyword matcher (ICF → toggle off slab + add ICF wall; insulation → add upgrade; solar → add PV) produces correct, specific, testable output for the three seeded templates. The whole chat UI, accept/reject flow, applySuggestion merger, and downstream applyCustomization are all real and exercised by the stub. The LLM swap is a one-function body replacement in a future llm-integration story.

factory_decisions.decision: Broken-dependency warning fires on DIRECT deps only, not transitive closure
factory_decisions.context: AC-TC-2: 'any downstream tasks that depended on it show a warning icon'. Could mean direct depends_on rows only, or full transitive closure (A→B→C, toggle A, flag both B and C).
alternatives_considered: Transitive closure via BFS over task_dependencies — O(V+E) per render, correct but hides the actual breakage point from the user
Flag nothing (leave cascade function to handle) — violates AC-TC-2 explicitly
Flag every task after the toggled task in order_index — ignores real deps, produces noise
factory_decisions.rationale: Direct-dep flagging has O(deps) complexity and shows the user exactly where a dep broke. As the user toggles off B (now flagged), C becomes flagged next — so transitive breakage surfaces naturally through iterative toggling rather than a single noisy warning cloud. Dormant in this story since template-apply doesn't write task_dependencies rows yet, but the UI is correct and tested for when deps are added in the task-management cycle.

factory_decisions.decision: Customization state held in client (React useState) until 'Create project' is clicked
factory_decisions.context: User toggles tasks + accepts suggestions across multiple interactions. Could persist optimistically on each change, or batch all changes into the final submit.
alternatives_considered: Persist every toggle via server action — N round trips, chat feels sluggish, partial-state DB rows if user abandons
localStorage draft like project-creation-draft — extra layer of state sync bugs for a flow the user finishes in one sitting
Supabase Realtime subscriptions for multi-device edit — overkill for single-user setup phase
factory_decisions.rationale: Customization is a single focused step on one device ending in 'Create project'. Client-only state means zero round trips during the customization itself, one round trip on submit (applyCustomization), one redirect (finalizeProject). If the user abandons, no partial DB state. Matches the browser-only draft pattern from TemplateBrowser.

factory_decisions.decision: Added tasks get planned_start = project.start_date on insert (not scheduled inline)
factory_decisions.context: applyCustomization inserts new tasks mid-schedule. Could: (a) recompute the cursor-based schedule across all tasks, (b) insert at project.start_date and let cascade_task_dates() refine, (c) compute a new planned_start based on the stage's existing task range.
alternatives_considered: Rebuild the cursor schedule for all tasks (delete+reinsert) — heavy, risks losing user-edited dates from later stories
Place new task inline at max(planned_end) within the stage — requires a pre-insert SELECT and still doesn't form proper dependencies
Leave planned_start null — violates tasks.planned_start NOT NULL schema constraint
factory_decisions.rationale: Start-date placeholder + cascade refinement is the pattern the schema was designed for: cascade_task_dates() is the single source of truth for date propagation. This story doesn't need to reinvent scheduling. The user can drag dates in the schedule-management story and cascade will propagate. NOT NULL satisfied; dates self-correct on first cascade.

factory_decisions.decision: Sequential date scheduling (no dependency graph) for template-apply
factory_decisions.context: Templates carry stages/tasks/materials but no task-dependency info. Template-apply needs to satisfy tasks.planned_start NOT NULL + planned_end NOT NULL on insert. Options: leave dates null (rejected by schema), chain linearly, or attempt to infer dependencies from task order.
alternatives_considered: Defer date computation to a separate cascade step (breaks schema NOT NULL constraints; would need migration)
Template carries explicit depends_on — requires reshaping seed JSONB and template-customization story handles dependency editing already
All tasks share project.start_date — loses the ability to show a meaningful schedule in the Gantt before customization
factory_decisions.rationale: Cursor-based linear schedule: planned_start of task N = planned_end of task N-1 + 1 day, planned_end = planned_start + duration_days. Produces a reasonable initial Gantt immediately after template apply; the user edits durations and dependencies in the template-customization + task-management stories and cascade_task_dates() refines from there. Pure function keeps the scheduling logic unit-testable without DB round-trips.

factory_decisions.decision: Server-side template summarization (summarizeTemplate) — send TemplateSummary to the browser, not the full JSONB
factory_decisions.context: Each template stores a fat stages JSONB (whole build with materials). Sending all 3 to the client for list + preview would waste ~15KB and block the 2s render target in AC-TB-4.
alternatives_considered: Ship raw templates array, summarize in the client — duplicates logic + bloats JS bundle
Paginate / lazy-load preview — overkill for 3 seeded templates
Render preview server-side with server actions — adds round-trip on every card click
factory_decisions.rationale: /setup computes TemplateSummary (stage/task counts, sample_tasks per stage, total_duration_days with computed fallback) in the RSC, passes only summaries to the TemplateBrowser client component. Apply still reads the full JSONB server-side via templateId. Keeps client payload small, server logic centralized, and preview switching instant.

factory_decisions.decision: Detect already-applied-template by counting stages on the project rather than a project flag
factory_decisions.context: After apply, the project is still status='setup' (customization story flips to 'active'). Navigating back to /setup needs to avoid letting the user apply a second template on top of the first.
alternatives_considered: Add a projects.template_id or projects.template_applied_at column — schema churn for a UI guard
Redirect to a dedicated /setup/customize route after apply — premature; customization story hasn't defined that route yet
Client-side localStorage flag — unreliable across devices
factory_decisions.rationale: A quick HEAD count on stages WHERE project_id = X is cheap (partial index exists), stateless, and inherently correct: if any stages exist, the template was applied. Works even if the user opened /setup on a second device. Zero schema changes.

factory_decisions.decision: Use local synthetic ids ('s0', 't0-1') in the insert plan to join inserts across three tables
factory_decisions.context: applyTemplate has to insert stages, then tasks (needing the stage's real id), then materials (needing the task's real id). supabase.insert().select() returns real ids but not in the same order as input, so a join key is required.
alternatives_considered: Insert stages one at a time to capture each id inline — N round trips instead of 1
Rely on array index ordering — Supabase does not guarantee it
Use Postgres CTE with WITH clauses — requires a stored procedure or rpc, more infra than needed
factory_decisions.rationale: Insert plan tags each row with a deterministic _localId ('s{order_index}', 't{stageIdx}-{taskIdx}'), and both stages.order_index and tasks.(stage_id + order_index) are carried through inserts. The server action rebuilds the local→real mapping from the returned rows after each insert. Three round trips total, correct by construction.

factory_decisions.context: AC-PS-1/2/3 involve branching validation (empty name, past date warning, confirmation flag). The form needs client-side validation for UX; the server action needs identical validation for safety. Two code paths = duplication risk.
alternatives_considered: Validate only server-side (requires round-trip for every validation error — poor mobile UX)
Validate only client-side (security hole — malicious clients can POST invalid data)
Schema library (zod) — another dep for 5 rules this simple
factory_decisions.rationale: Pure functions accepting (values, today) return { errors, warnings }. Used by both the form and the server action. 13 unit tests run in <10ms, no mocks needed. McKinley: boring technology — plain TypeScript, no runtime deps.

factory_decisions.decision: Past-date confirmation via checkbox inside an amber warning panel (not browser confirm())
factory_decisions.context: AC-PS-3 requires 'explicit confirmation' for past start_date. Options: native window.confirm(), inline checkbox, two-step submit.
alternatives_considered: window.confirm() — blocking modal, inaccessible, ugly on mobile
Two-step submit (first click shows warning, second submits) — confusing UX, easy to double-click through
Modal dialog — overkill for a single confirmation
factory_decisions.rationale: Inline amber panel with a checkbox: visible, accessible, preserves form context, prevents accidental submits. Confirmation state resets whenever the date changes so the user re-confirms if they adjust.

factory_decisions.decision: localStorage draft persistence stored under versioned key 'cm:project-creation-draft:v1'
factory_decisions.context: AC-PS-5: form restore across sessions. Need a key that won't collide with other drafts and can be migrated.
alternatives_considered: sessionStorage (lost on tab close, fails AC-PS-5)
IndexedDB (overkill for 3 string fields)
Unversioned key 'project-draft' (can't migrate schema without orphaning old drafts)
factory_decisions.rationale: Namespaced + versioned key allows future form schema changes. Draft is cleared after successful createProject redirect (returns to same page, effect unmounts, clearDraft called in the success path).

factory_decisions.decision: Cast-at-site for projects.insert() rather than editing lib/supabase/types.ts
factory_decisions.context: Foundation eval flagged lib/supabase/types.ts as lacking Relationships[] on every table, which causes typed Postgrest inserts to resolve to 'never'. Options: regenerate types, add Relationships: [] to all 12 tables, or cast at call sites.
alternatives_considered: Edit all 12 tables to add Relationships: [] — touches 12 tables for a single insert, high blast radius
Regenerate types via supabase gen types — correct long-term fix but requires Supabase CLI auth and migration roundtrip, out of scope for this story
Disable strict typing globally — defeats the purpose
factory_decisions.rationale: Single targeted cast in app/actions/projects.ts with a comment pointing at the foundation-eval finding. The typed Row/Insert shapes are still used by app/page.tsx and app/(app)/setup/page.tsx for reads. A dedicated types-regeneration pass belongs in a separate chore, not buried inside a feature story.

factory_decisions.decision: Removed next-pwa, wrote custom service worker
factory_decisions.context: next-pwa v5 injects webpack config, incompatible with Next.js 16 Turbopack default. Build failed with 'This build is using Turbopack, with a webpack config and no turbopack config.'
alternatives_considered: @ducanh2912/next-pwa — Turbopack-compatible fork, but adds another dependency for a problem solvable with 80 lines of vanilla JS
next build --webpack — disables Turbopack, slower builds, not idiomatic for Next.js 16
factory_decisions.rationale: A custom sw.js at public/sw.js served with no-cache headers is the recommended Next.js 16 approach. No Workbox dependency, full control over caching strategy, easier to debug.

factory_decisions.decision: Supabase project 'construction-coordinator' (***REMOVED***) used for construction-manager
factory_decisions.context: Git branch [isolation-recovery: this decision was reverted — construction-coordinator matched both the Supabase project name and Vercel project name. ACTIVE_HEALTHY status confirmed.
alternatives_considered: Create new Supabase project — would require pausing an existing project (at 2/2 free tier limit) and waiting for activation
Use 'openfleet' project — wrong project, different product
factory_decisions.rationale: The naming alignment (construction-coordinator project + construction-coordinator Supabase + construction-coordinator Vercel project) is unambiguous. Project was pre-created as part of the build setup.

factory_decisions.decision: webpush.setVapidDetails() moved inside handler functions
factory_decisions.context: Calling setVapidDetails at module evaluation time caused build failure on Vercel: 'No key set in vapidDetails.privateKey'. Turbopack evaluates route modules during static page generation.
alternatives_considered: Lazy initialization singleton — adds unnecessary complexity
Dynamic import of web-push — works but slower
factory_decisions.rationale: Moving setVapidDetails inside the handler is the simplest fix. It runs once per cold start in production (Fluid Compute reuses instances). No performance impact.

factory_decisions.decision: cascade_task_dates() implemented as PostgreSQL function with recursive CTE
factory_decisions.context: Cascade engine spec requires single-transaction update of all downstream tasks. Acceptable: under 2s for 500 tasks.
alternatives_considered: Application-layer BFS in TypeScript — multiple round trips, not atomic, slower for large graphs
PostgreSQL stored procedure with LOOP — not set-based, harder to optimize
factory_decisions.rationale: Recursive CTE traverses the full DAG in one query, updates all affected tasks + materials atomically. PostgreSQL can optimize the traversal with the existing task_dependencies indexes.

factory_decisions.decision: shift_alerts table for briefing delta tracking
factory_decisions.context: Daily briefing needs to show 'what changed since last visit'. Options: compute on-the-fly (expensive join), event log (append-only), or client-side localStorage.
alternatives_considered: On-the-fly computation — requires tracking last_visit_at per user and joining across all cascades
localStorage — lost on device switch, not reliable for mobile
Supabase Realtime subscription — overkill for a daily summary
factory_decisions.rationale: Server-side event log (shift_alerts) written by the cascade function. Dismissed flag per user. Readable from any device. Foundation builds the table; daily-experience feature cycle builds the UI.

factory_decisions.decision: StageManager uses client-side optimistic updates with server-side reorderProjectStages as the persistence truth
factory_decisions.context: AC-SM-2 requires drag reorder to re-render the Gantt "with the new ordering." Options: (a) drag → server round trip → server returns new list → re-render; (b) client reorders locally, server action confirms in background; (c) server action re-throws errors and force-reverts.
alternatives_considered: Server-first: every drag waits for the server — drag feels laggy, breaks the direct-manipulation UX spec
Optimistic + rollback on failure: requires snapshot + restore logic; adds state management for a rare failure path
Supabase Realtime subscription: overkill for single-user project editing
factory_decisions.rationale: Drag updates the local stages state immediately (pure reorderStages produces the renumbered array), then fires reorderProjectStages in a startTransition. If the server fails, serverError banner shows and the user can retry. Single-device single-user editing means conflict is vanishingly rare; the optimistic path keeps drag feeling instant. Matches the client-only draft pattern from ProjectCreationForm + CustomizationScreen.

factory_decisions.decision: Up/down arrow buttons shipped alongside native HTML5 drag-and-drop
factory_decisions.context: AC-SM-2 says "drags a stage to reorder" but this is a PWA and touch devices (iOS Safari) do not reliably fire HTML5 drag events. The alternatives are a heavier touch-drag library (react-dnd, dnd-kit), omitting mobile support, or providing a parallel keyboard/tap interaction.
alternatives_considered: Install dnd-kit or react-dnd for unified touch+mouse drag — new dependency, ~40KB, another abstraction to learn, McKinley says no unless drag is core
Ship drag-only, leave mobile broken — violates PWA product standard; stage reorder is core enough that mobile parity matters
Polyfill TouchEvent → DragEvent — fragile, hand-rolled, adds more bugs than it solves
factory_decisions.rationale: Boring-tech drag via draggable=true covers desktop (the primary Gantt-editing environment) at zero cost. The up/down arrow buttons next to each stage row give touch users the same reorder capability through the same reorderStages() pure helper and reorderProjectStages server action. One feature, two input modalities, one code path. When the Gantt story needs true 2-D drag we can swap in dnd-kit then.

factory_decisions.decision: Two-phase reorder update: tombstone-with-negative-indices then write final slots
factory_decisions.context: reorderProjectStages writes a new order_index to every stage in a project. Today there is no UNIQUE(project_id, order_index) constraint, so a one-pass update would work. But partial densification (e.g., swapping s1 order 0↔1 with s2 order 1) could briefly assign two rows the same index during the sequence of updates.
alternatives_considered: Single-pass update with arbitrary order: works today, breaks silently if a UNIQUE constraint is ever added
Supabase RPC with a single transactional UPDATE ... FROM: requires a new migration + stored procedure for a 4-stage reorder
Delete-and-reinsert: loses stage ids, cascades tasks away
factory_decisions.rationale: Two passes — first push each stage to order_index = -(1 + position) as a tombstone, then write the final 0..N-1 index — guarantees no two rows ever share a positive order_index mid-update. No new infrastructure, cheap (2N updates for N stages, N≤~12 typical), and safe if a UNIQUE constraint shows up in a later migration. deleteStage uses the same densification pattern on remaining stages.

factory_decisions.decision: Delete confirmation driven by client-side task_count, server independently re-verifies
factory_decisions.context: AC-SM-3 vs AC-SM-4 gates on "stage has tasks." The page loads per-stage task counts; the client uses them to decide whether to show the dialog. But the server action also needs to decide whether to allow an unconfirmed delete.
alternatives_considered: Client-only decision, server always deletes when called: a stale/tampered client could delete a populated stage without confirmation
Server returns requiresConfirmation the first call, client retries with confirmed=true: doubles round trips for every delete
Skip the dialog entirely, always server-delete with cascade: loses AC-SM-3 warning requirement
factory_decisions.rationale: Client shows the dialog based on the task_count it loaded (fresh render = fresh count). Server action independently re-counts tasks — if taskCount > 0 and confirmed=false, it returns requiresConfirmation without deleting. The client ignores that fallback path in the happy case (it already showed the dialog) but the server guarantee means a stale count or a rogue client cannot delete populated stages without explicit confirmed=true. Defense in depth, one round trip in the common case.

factory_decisions.decision: Six-swatch color palette (hardcoded) instead of free-form color picker
factory_decisions.context: AC-SM-1 specifies a "color" field. Options: free-form <input type="color">, preset palette, hex text input.
alternatives_considered: Native <input type="color">: gives users infinite options that can clash with the brand palette; color wheel UI is inconsistent across browsers
Hex text input: requires validation copy + error UX for every typo
Larger preset (12+ swatches): decision paralysis, the Gantt only needs enough contrast to tell stages apart
factory_decisions.rationale: Six swatches drawn from the product's brand palette (earth brown, frame green, ochre, ink, slate blue, brick red) give every stage a distinct, on-brand color. Picker UI is a row of circular buttons with aria-pressed — accessible, instant, no typing. validateStageInput still accepts any valid hex so a future "custom color" story (or a direct DB edit) is not blocked. normalizeStageColor falls back to the earth-brown default if the stored value is ever corrupt.

factory_decisions.decision: Inclusive-day semantics for planned_start/planned_end (end = start + duration − 1)
factory_decisions.context: AC-TM-2 says "planned_end = planned_start + 2 days" for a 2-day task. Two interpretations: exclusive (start=06-01, end=06-03 → 2 nights) or inclusive (start=06-01, end=06-02 → first and last working day, 2 working days).
alternatives_considered: Exclusive: planned_end = planned_start + duration_days. Simpler arithmetic but a 1-day task would render as a zero-width Gantt bar and the end date is a day the task is NOT worked.
Inclusive: planned_end = planned_start + (duration_days − 1). Matches how humans read a schedule ("Monday–Tuesday = 2 days") and matches the existing seed data pattern.
factory_decisions.rationale: Checked the seeded O'Brien data + the template-apply cursor schedule — both treat planned_start and planned_end as the first and last working day of the task (inclusive). A 1-day task has planned_start = planned_end. The cursor schedule in lib/templates/apply.ts advances via `start = prev.planned_end + 1`, which is exactly AC-TM-4's "max(dep.planned_end) + 1 day" formula — consistent across the codebase.

factory_decisions.decision: AC-TM-3 "no deps AND no prior task" resolves to project.start_date for all no-deps cases
factory_decisions.context: AC-TM-3 specifies the no-deps-no-prior-task case but is silent on no-deps-WITH-prior-tasks-in-stage. template-apply uses a sequential cursor (each task follows the previous one in order_index). template-customization uses project.start_date as a placeholder.
alternatives_considered: Sequential cursor within the stage — infers an implicit dependency chain the user did not author. If the user really wants sequence, they can set depends_on explicitly.
Max end of prior tasks in stage — leaks "stage order" into date computation. Same problem: implicit chain.
project.start_date uniformly when depends_on is empty — predictable, self-documenting, matches the customization-story placeholder pattern.
factory_decisions.rationale: Dependencies are the single source of truth for sequencing. If a user adds a task with no dependencies, they get project.start_date and can fix it by either adding deps or editing the date directly in a later story. Explicit > implicit. Matches the template-customization decision to use start_date as the placeholder for newly-added tasks.

factory_decisions.decision: AC-TM-6 dependency cleanup relies on ON DELETE CASCADE on task_dependencies.depends_on_task_id
factory_decisions.context: The spec requires that deleting task A removes it from the depends_on array of every downstream task. Options: handle in application code, use the DB cascade, or both.
alternatives_considered: Application-layer: SELECT dependents, DELETE by task_id = A, then DELETE rows where depends_on_task_id = A. Three round trips, race conditions if another request is editing deps simultaneously.
DB cascade only: task_dependencies has ON DELETE CASCADE on BOTH foreign keys (task_id, depends_on_task_id). Deleting task A atomically removes every row that references A in either column.
Both layers for defense in depth: duplicates effort without improving guarantees.
factory_decisions.rationale: The schema already guarantees AC-TM-6 via CASCADE. Server action just deletes the task and RLS + CASCADE do the rest. Added a pure removeFromDependencies() helper for the UI to mirror the same behaviour in client state — so the task list re-renders correctly without a round trip.

factory_decisions.decision: updateTaskDuration holds planned_start fixed and recalculates only planned_end
factory_decisions.context: AC-TM-5 says "edits the duration_days field and saves, THEN the task's planned_end recalculates and cascades." Options: recompute planned_start from deps then extend end, or keep start stable and extend/shrink end.
alternatives_considered: Recompute planned_start from deps on every duration edit — could shift the start date if deps have changed since task creation, surprising the user when they just edited duration.
Hold start fixed, shift end only — matches the AC's "end recalculates" wording and is what cascade_task_dates() is designed for (it receives new_planned_end and propagates the delta downstream).
Re-run the full schedule — massive blast radius for a single edit.
factory_decisions.rationale: cascade_task_dates(p_task_id, p_new_planned_start, p_new_planned_end) expects the caller to pick the new end. Passing the current planned_start unchanged + new planned_end = start + (duration − 1) gives a clean delta the recursive CTE uses to shift every downstream task by the same offset. One RPC, one transaction, O(downstream) updates.

factory_decisions.decision: Add Task lives on /stages/:id, reached via an "Open" link on each stage row in /schedule
factory_decisions.context: AC-TM-1 says "clicks Add Task within [a stage]". Three possible homes: inline on /schedule (expand each stage), dedicated /stages/:id detail, or a full modal launched from the schedule list.
alternatives_considered: Inline-on-schedule: every stage on the schedule page expands into a task list. Crowds a Gantt-like screen; doesn't scale past ~3 tasks per stage.
/stages/:id detail page with full task CRUD: clean single-purpose screen, future-proof for per-stage editing, and the /stages/:id route already exists as a placeholder.
Global /tasks list: loses the stage scoping AC-TM-1 requires.
factory_decisions.rationale: The foundation already scaffolded /stages/[id]/page.tsx as a placeholder route and the /schedule StageManager already has per-stage rows. Adding an "Open" link to each row + rewriting the stage detail page as the task-CRUD screen uses existing infrastructure, keeps /schedule lightweight, and gives tasks a natural home for future attributes (materials, photos, trade assignment).

factory_decisions.decision: Trade delete uses explicit unassign-then-delete (no SET NULL FK)
factory_decisions.context: AC-TR-4 requires deleting a trade assigned to N tasks to null out task.trade_id for all N tasks. Options: rely on a SET NULL FK, run an UPDATE then DELETE in the server action, or wrap in an RPC/transaction.
alternatives_considered: Add ON DELETE SET NULL to tasks.trade_id FK — requires a new migration for a behaviour the UI already needs to orchestrate around a confirmation dialog; the schema is shared with other stories, migration churn is expensive.
Single DELETE with a pre-count and trust a future FK cascade — if the FK is ever added with RESTRICT (the default), the delete fails silently for assigned trades.
RPC wrapping both updates in a transaction — overkill for a two-statement sequence that can't meaningfully partially-fail at this scale.
factory_decisions.rationale: UPDATE tasks SET trade_id=null WHERE trade_id=X AND project_id=P, then DELETE FROM trades WHERE id=X. Two statements, both scoped by project_id so cross-project writes are impossible, no schema change, no RPC. Server recounts assignments independently before acting on confirmed=true, so a stale client cannot bypass the confirmation dialog.

factory_decisions.decision: Trade assignment on /tasks/:id is an inline optimistic <select>, not a modal
factory_decisions.context: AC-TR-2 says 'assigns it to a task via task edit form.' The existing Add Task form already has a trade dropdown; the detail screen needs a standalone control for re-assigning an existing task.
alternatives_considered: Open a full edit-task modal from the detail page — duplicates StageTasksManager's add form, and this story's scope is trade-contacts not task-edit.
Inline 'Edit' button that opens an edit drawer with every task field — scope creep into task-management follow-up work already flagged in factory_questions.
Dedicated /tasks/:id/edit route — extra navigation step for a single-field change.
factory_decisions.rationale: A single <select> element on the detail page changes trade_id in-place with an optimistic client update + server rollback on error. Matches the lightweight direct-manipulation pattern from StageManager's drag-reorder and avoids opening a form just to change one field. Other fields (name/notes/duration) are explicitly deferred to a future task-edit story per the existing factory_question.

factory_decisions.decision: formatTelHref strips all non-digit characters but preserves a single leading +
factory_decisions.context: AC-TR-3 requires 'a tap-to-call link using the tel: protocol.' Trade phone numbers are free-text — Irish mobile ('087 123 4567'), Irish landline with area code ('01 4567890'), international ('+353 1 456 7890'), US-style with parens ('(555) 123-4567'). tel: URIs accept digits and a leading + (RFC 3966).
alternatives_considered: Pass the raw string through to tel: — spaces and parens break dialer parsing on some Android builds and produce inconsistent behaviour across platforms.
Full RFC 3966 parser with country-code inference — overkill; we don't know the user's default country and most dialers DTRT with digits+leading-plus.
Refuse to build a link unless the input matches a strict regex — rejects valid numbers users type naturally, degrades AC-TR-3 to nothing.
factory_decisions.rationale: Strip to digits, preserve leading + if present. Returns null if no digits remain so the caller can render the phone as plain text instead of a broken link. Tested against Irish mobile/landline, international +353, US (555) formats. Keeps the raw phone string in the DB for display; only the href is normalized.

factory_decisions.decision: Assignment counts loaded server-side on /trades (no per-row query)
factory_decisions.context: The trade list needs per-trade assignment counts to render the delete warning with the right task count without a server round trip on every delete attempt. Options: N queries (one per trade), SQL GROUP BY via an RPC, or a single broad select.
alternatives_considered: One count() query per trade — N round trips for N trades; slow with any number of trades.
Custom rpc get_trade_assignment_counts() — new stored procedure for what one select can do.
Client-side assignment count derived from a tasks fetch — doubles the client payload for a page that doesn't otherwise need tasks.
factory_decisions.rationale: One SELECT trade_id FROM tasks WHERE project_id=P AND trade_id IS NOT NULL, then tally into a Map. One round trip, O(tasks) memory on the server, zero schema change. Projects won't realistically have thousands of tasks in this target domain (residential self-build).

factory_decisions.decision: order_by_date computed client+server from task.planned_start - lead_time_days, mirroring the DB trigger
factory_decisions.context: AC-ML-4 requires that editing lead_time_days recalculates order_by_date. The schema's cascade_task_dates trigger already computes this on task-date changes; the question was whether the material form should POST a pre-computed order_by_date or rely on the trigger.
alternatives_considered: Set order_by_date=NULL on insert/update and let a DB trigger derive it — requires a new BEFORE-INSERT/UPDATE trigger, more SQL infra for a one-line calculation
Run a follow-up UPDATE after insert that calls a stored proc — two round trips per save
Derive order_by_date at read time in the RSC — ignores the existing schema column and diverges from the cascade trigger
factory_decisions.rationale: computeOrderByDate() is a pure TypeScript function identical to the SQL the cascade trigger runs (planned_start minus lead_time_days). The server action computes it once on insert/update so the value stored matches what the trigger would overwrite on the next cascade. One round trip, single source of truth for the formula, easy to unit test with UTC date arithmetic.

factory_decisions.decision: Materials live inline on /tasks/:id as a TaskMaterialsManager section (not a dedicated /materials/:id route)
factory_decisions.context: AC-ML-1 says 'clicks Add Material on the task detail screen, THEN an inline form appears'. Affected screens also list /materials but no AC describes what belongs there. Choice: inline section, modal, or dedicated subroute.
alternatives_considered: Modal dialog like the trade add/edit flow — heavier UI for what the spec calls 'inline form'
Dedicated /tasks/:id/materials subroute — extra navigation step, breaks the 'everything about this task on one screen' mental model
Leave on the /materials list page — contradicts AC-ML-1's 'task detail screen' wording
factory_decisions.rationale: A section within /tasks/:id with an inline expandable form matches AC-ML-1 literally, keeps the task as the container for its materials (which is how the data model is scoped: materials.task_id NOT NULL), and reuses the existing TaskTradeAssigner layout on that page. The /materials index remains the future home for a cross-task materials dashboard (ordering, deliveries, inventory) — that belongs to a separate story.

factory_decisions.decision: lead_time_days validation collapses empty, negative, non-integer, and non-numeric inputs to a single error message
factory_decisions.context: AC-ML-3 specifies exactly one message ('Lead time must be 0 or more days') for 'empty or negative'. The input is a text field (so the user can also type 'abc' or '2.5'). Choice: multiple messages per failure mode, or one message that covers them all.
alternatives_considered: Separate messages for 'must be a number' / 'must be a whole number' / 'must be 0 or more' — more precise, but three different disabled-save states and more test surface
Silently coerce bad input (parseInt + floor) — changes the user's intent and hides typos
factory_decisions.rationale: The AC calls out exactly one copy string. Collapsing every invalid-input variant under that message keeps behaviour consistent with the AC verbatim, keeps the save-button-disabled logic simple (liveValidation.ok), and the HTML type=number input already filters most non-numeric keystrokes on keyboards that support it.

factory_decisions.decision: Save button disable is driven by a live validation on every render, not an onChange-computed error state
factory_decisions.context: AC-ML-3 says 'the save button is disabled' while lead_time is invalid. Two ways to express that: (a) recompute validity inside render, or (b) store an isValid flag and update it in each field's onChange.
alternatives_considered: onChange-synced isValid boolean + useEffect — adds a render + stale-state risk when form initializes on edit
Debounced validation — unnecessary for a synchronous pure function
Only validate on submit — would require leaving the save button enabled until click, contradicting AC-ML-3 literally
factory_decisions.rationale: validateMaterialInput is a pure O(1) function; calling it once per render is free and guarantees the disabled state always reflects the current form values, including when the form is first populated by Edit. formErrors is still only set on submit or server-error, so the user doesn't see red text until they try to save.

factory_decisions.decision: Include overdue not_ordered materials in /briefing Upcoming Orders (top of the sorted list)
factory_decisions.context: AC-OB-4 literal wording is "order_by_date within the next 7 days, sorted ascending". Taken strictly this excludes overdue items, but AC-OB-2 says overdue materials must render somewhere on /briefing or /materials, and hiding them from the briefing defeats the purpose of the section for a foreman planning their day.
alternatives_considered: Strictly [today, today+7] — hides overdue items from briefing entirely; foreman misses urgent orders
Separate "Overdue" subsection above Upcoming Orders — adds UI surface, duplicates the badge signal
Include order_by_date <= today+7 (chosen) — overdue items sort to the top by ISO date, one list, badge colors disambiguate
factory_decisions.rationale: selectUpcomingOrders filters to not_ordered + order_by_date <= today+7. Ascending ISO sort naturally places overdue dates first, and the red overdue badge disambiguates them from the amber due-soon items. One list, zero duplication, aligned with AC-OB-2 requirement that overdue materials appear in /briefing.

factory_decisions.decision: Badge boundaries: overdue = order_by_date < today; due_soon = 0 <= days <= 7 (inclusive both ends)
factory_decisions.context: AC-OB-2 says "in the past", AC-OB-3 says "within the next 7 days". "In the past" is unambiguous but "within the next 7 days" could mean [today, today+7] or [today+1, today+7]. Also: should the boundary at day 7 be inclusive?
alternatives_considered: Exclusive upper bound (< 7 days) — a material due exactly 7 days out would show no badge, then flip to due_soon tomorrow — jarring UX
Start window at today+1 — a material due today shows no badge despite being the most urgent pending item
factory_decisions.rationale: Inclusive on both ends matches "within the next 7 days" as a foreman reads it. day-diff >= 0 && <= 7 → due_soon, day-diff < 0 → overdue. Stable transitions day-to-day.

factory_decisions.decision: Deadline status is computed at render time from "today" + order_by_date, not persisted
factory_decisions.context: Badges change daily as today advances past each order_by_date. Options: (a) store a computed badge column + recompute via cron, (b) compute at render time from the existing order_by_date column.
alternatives_considered: Store badge state in a column + nightly cron — extra table columns, an extra cron job, drift between DB and reality between runs
Compute in a DB view — needs view maintenance, SQL time() calls vary by connection timezone
factory_decisions.rationale: materialDeadlineStatus is a ~10 line pure function. Computing at render time in the RSC guarantees correctness at every page load, with no background job, no extra columns, no drift. The only input is today's date (UTC) and the already-persisted order_by_date.

factory_decisions.decision: Status transitions are strictly forward: not_ordered→ordered→delivered (no rollback UI)
factory_decisions.context: AC-MS-1/AC-MS-2 describe forward actions only (Mark Ordered, Mark Delivered) and the filter tabs imply the statuses are terminal states. No AC covers un-ordering or un-delivering.
alternatives_considered: Allow any→any transition with a dropdown — scope creep, no AC requires it, risks accidental status reset
Allow reverse transitions via an explicit Undo button — more UI surface without AC coverage
factory_decisions.rationale: updateMaterialStatus enforces valid transitions on the server (not_ordered→ordered, ordered→delivered, plus no-op re-assert for idempotency). Delivered is terminal. If a user mis-clicks, the material can still be deleted + re-added. A future story can add correction workflows if real-world usage shows mis-clicks happen often.

factory_decisions.decision: MaterialStatusBadge replaces (not overlays) MaterialDeadlineBadge once order_status != not_ordered
factory_decisions.context: AC-MS-1 says the green 'ordered' badge 'replaces any deadline badges'. Two badge slots or one?
alternatives_considered: Show both badges in parallel — a not_ordered + overdue material that just got ordered would briefly show both, confusing
Keep deadline badge visible alongside status — contradicts AC-MS-1 'replaces' wording and clutters the row
factory_decisions.rationale: Single badge slot per row, chosen by order_status: not_ordered → deadline badge (overdue/due_soon/null), ordered/delivered → status badge. Matches AC-MS-1 literally and materialDeadlineStatus already returns null for anything other than not_ordered, so the deadline is definitionally irrelevant after ordering.

factory_decisions.decision: Filter tab state lives in a client component (MaterialsListView), not the URL
factory_decisions.context: AC-MS-3 requires filter tabs on /materials that show only matching rows. State could live in URL search params (shareable, bookmarkable) or local client state (simpler).
alternatives_considered: URL search param ?status=ordered — shareable but requires a Next.js searchParams flow, a client router.push on every tab click, and a full RSC re-fetch per tab
Server-side filter via a prop on the page — would require searchParams; same ergonomics issue
factory_decisions.rationale: Owner-builder uses /materials as a live dashboard, not a bookmarkable report. All rows are already fetched (materials list is small — ~10-50 per project), so client-side filter is instant with no round-trip. Filter tab reset on navigation is acceptable behavior (matches every tabbed UI the user has seen). Counts per tab are memoized from the same array.

factory_decisions.decision: Cascade summary layer is a pure TS module over the existing PG function
factory_decisions.context: cascade_task_dates() was already implemented as a plpgsql recursive-CTE function by the initial schema migration. It owns the transactional update (AC-CE-1, AC-CE-2, AC-CE-5) and returns downstream changes only. AC-CE-3 requires a summary listing ALL tasks that moved — including the trigger task — with old and new dates.
alternatives_considered: Rewrite cascade_task_dates to include the trigger task in its RETURN TABLE — would require a new SQL migration and coordinate with the existing callers (customization.ts, materials/operations.ts)
Compute the full summary inside the server action ad-hoc — duplicates logic across updateTaskDuration and any future date-drag UI
Ship the downstream-only result to clients and have the UI prepend the trigger — mixes presentation with transaction shape, hard to unit-test
factory_decisions.rationale: lib/cascade/engine.ts is a pure module: computeCascadeDelta, shiftTaskDates, buildCascadeSummary. 13 unit tests covering delta math, month-boundaries, zero-downstream case (AC-CE-4), trigger-as-first-row, and identical delta on every row. Server actions call buildCascadeSummary with (trigger row from pre-update SELECT, downstream rows from RPC return). Zero SQL churn, testable without a DB.

factory_decisions.decision: delta_days is carried on every movement row, not just the summary header
factory_decisions.context: buildCascadeSummary returns both summary.delta_days (once) and movement.delta_days (per row). Options: (a) header only, (b) per row, (c) both.
alternatives_considered: Header only — the UI still needs to show "+5d" next to each task-moved toast; callers recompute per row
Per row only — loses the single-number summary for a toast like "5 tasks moved +5 days"
factory_decisions.rationale: Both is cheap (all rows share one value) and lets the UI render a header badge AND per-row chips from the same payload. The duplicate data is a pure function of the trigger delta — no drift risk.

factory_decisions.decision: Delay validation rejects same-date and earlier dates (forward-only push)
factory_decisions.context: AC-DL-2 says 'user selects a later date'. Ambiguous on equality: does a same-date apply count as a valid no-op? Also unclear whether pulling the date in (earlier) counts as delay logging.
alternatives_considered: Accept same-date as a no-op + show a 'No changes' toast — wastes a cascade RPC round trip and confuses the user about whether the delay was recorded
Accept any date (earlier or later) — conflates delay logging with duration editing, which already exists as updateTaskDuration and handles pull-in semantics
Silently ignore equal dates on the client — hides the validation failure and disables the Apply button without explanation
factory_decisions.rationale: validateDelayDate requires strictly newPlannedEnd > currentPlannedEnd. The Apply button disables with an inline error ('New date must be after the current end date') when the user picks the same or an earlier date. Forward-only matches the user mental model of 'log a delay' and keeps the feature orthogonal to updateTaskDuration (which handles both forward and backward duration changes).

factory_decisions.decision: Materials-moved count derived post-cascade by counting materials on affected task ids
factory_decisions.context: AC-DL-3 requires the overlay to show M material order-by dates moved. cascade_task_dates() updates material order_by_date in-place but its RETURN TABLE only reports downstream TASKS, not materials. Options: (a) rewrite the RPC to return material counts, (b) count materials attached to affected tasks in a follow-up SELECT, (c) compute in the RSC on next page load.
alternatives_considered: Modify the PG function to return material_count — new SQL migration, breaks existing updateTaskDuration callers that expect only downstream task rows
Render the count on the next /briefing load — the overlay must be immediate per AC-DL-3, cross-navigation is a different UX
Track material updates via a DB trigger logging to shift_alerts — shift_alerts population is deferred to the daily-briefing cycle per existing factory_question
factory_decisions.rationale: After cascade_task_dates returns the downstream task ids, a single SELECT COUNT on materials WHERE task_id IN (trigger + downstream) gives M. The PG function updates exactly those materials (see initial_schema.sql lines 459-485), so the count is precise. One extra round trip, no SQL churn, no coordination with updateTaskDuration callers.

factory_decisions.decision: Delay picker lives on /tasks/:id as an inline expandable section, not a modal or /briefing button
factory_decisions.context: AC-DL-1 says the picker opens from /tasks/:id OR /briefing. /briefing currently renders Upcoming Orders only (no task list). Options: inline section on /tasks/:id, modal dialog, add a tasks section to /briefing, or both.
alternatives_considered: Modal dialog — heavier UI for a single-field form; matches the cascade summary overlay but doubles the overlay layers
Add a Today's Tasks section to /briefing with a Log Delay button per row — expands the briefing scope beyond what AC-DL-1 requires and overlaps with a future daily-briefing story
Combined inline + briefing buttons — duplicates UI surface and doubles the QA matrix
factory_decisions.rationale: AC-DL-1 wording is OR — one location satisfies it. /tasks/:id is the natural home: the task's other per-task controls (trade assigner, materials manager) already live there, the planned_end is already shown in the header, and an inline expandable section matches the in-place-edit pattern from TaskTradeAssigner. A Log Delay button on /briefing can be added in a follow-up briefing story when the tasks section is designed.

factory_decisions.decision: Summary overlay is a modal dialog rendered by the same client component, closed on user action
factory_decisions.context: AC-DL-3 says a summary overlay displays after cascade completes. Options: modal (fixed inset), toast notification, inline expansion of the delay section, or RSC re-render of the page with summary in the URL.
alternatives_considered: Toast notification — too small to list the affected tasks; toasts are for transient confirmations not actionable lists
Inline expansion — collapses the picker and expands a summary in place; acceptable but makes it easy to miss the cascade scale on a long detail page
URL param + page re-render — requires router.push + searchParams plumbing for what is ephemeral UI state
factory_decisions.rationale: fixed-inset modal overlay with role=dialog + aria-modal=true guarantees the user sees the cascade scope immediately and has to acknowledge it (Done button). It lists every affected task (trigger + downstream) with old -> new dates and delta per row. State is local to TaskDelayLogger so no cross-component coordination. Closed via the Done button; overlay data is transient.

factory_decisions.decision: Builder-defined acceptance criteria for cascade-material-sync (spec was empty)
factory_decisions.context: state.json story entry for cascade-material-sync has no acceptance_criteria field — only name, affected_entities (Material), affected_screens (/materials, /briefing), depends_on (order-by-calculation, cascade-engine). story_context.spec was just {id, name}. Builder phase prompt says: ambiguity → check context, make decision, log as factory_decision.
alternatives_considered: Block the story with a factory_question asking a human to write ACs — defeats the autonomous loop, and the story name + screens are specific enough to synthesize a coherent contract.
Inherit cascade-engine ACs (AC-CE-*) — those are about task cascade, not material sync; would duplicate coverage.

Treat the story as already complete (SQL already syncs materials) and skip — ignores the UI side of the "sync" (surfacing which materials moved in the overlay + revalidating /materials and /briefing).
factory_decisions.rationale: Defined AC-CMS-1 (pure helper mirrors SQL material-update math), AC-CMS-2 (server actions return MaterialMovement[] not just a count), AC-CMS-3 (revalidate /materials on cascade), AC-CMS-4 (revalidate /briefing on cascade). These four criteria directly map to the story name (Cascade Material Order-By Sync) and the two affected_screens. Each AC has tests or a concrete revalidatePath call. QA can verify all four against seeded data.

factory_decisions.decision: Mirror SQL cascade_task_dates() material math in TypeScript (computeMaterialMovements) rather than read order_by_date back from DB
factory_decisions.context: The SQL cascade already writes the new order_by_date value in the same transaction. Two ways to surface per-material movements to the client: (a) after RPC, re-SELECT materials — get new value, lose old value; (b) mirror the math in JS using each task old/new planned_start + lead_time_days.
alternatives_considered: Read order_by_date before + after the RPC — two extra round-trips, race with any concurrent cascade.
Extend cascade_task_dates() to RETURN material rows — bigger surface area, needs migration, changes the RPC contract cascade-engine/delay-logging already depend on.
Skip per-material movements, keep the count-only summary — loses fidelity; the user can see which tasks shifted but not which specific materials they need to rush orders for.
factory_decisions.rationale: computeMaterialMovements is a pure function sharing computeOrderByDate with lib/materials/operations.ts — same math the DB trigger and UI-predict path already use, so the three layers cannot diverge. Server action loads material metadata once (id/name/lead_time/task_id), builds old/new start map from RPC response, and computes both old and new order_by_date locally. No second DB read, no race, testable without mocks. Count of "materials moved" excludes zero-delta rows so the trigger task's own materials (whose planned_start is unchanged in both current cascade paths) do not inflate the number.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for dependency-validation story
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 0 BUILD, 2 EXTEND, 1 REUSE, 0 REFACTOR-THEN-BUILD tasks. Key reuse: lib/tasks/dependency-graph.ts already contained detectCycle, formatCyclePath, removeStaleDependencies (all 18 tests passing). Extended: app/actions/tasks.ts (added addDependency/removeDependency/cleanStaleDependencies server actions), components/tasks/StageTasksManager.tsx (added Deps button + dep edit modal), app/(app)/stages/[id]/page.tsx (added stale dep cleanup on load).

factory_decisions.decision: Cycle detection gates addDependency (existing tasks), not createTask (new tasks)
factory_decisions.context: AC-DV-1 says 'when the user attempts to add A to B's depends_on array'. For a brand-new task with no ID yet, no other task can depend on it, so adding deps to a new task cannot create cycles. The cycle-detection gate belongs on the addDependency server action for existing tasks.
alternatives_considered: Gate createTask with cycle detection — cycles are impossible for new tasks since no other task references them yet. Would add unnecessary DB queries on every task creation.
Only validate in the UI (client-side) — the server must be the authority to prevent bypass via API calls.
factory_decisions.rationale: addDependency loads all project edges, runs detectCycle (O(V+E) DFS), and rejects with the formatted cycle path. createTask skips the check because the new task is a DAG leaf by definition. Server-side validation prevents bypass.

factory_decisions.decision: Stale dep cleanup on page load (RSC) rather than a background cron or explicit user action
factory_decisions.context: AC-DV-2 says 'WHEN the task is loaded, THEN the stale ID is automatically removed'. Options: cleanup on page load, background cron, explicit user action.
alternatives_considered: Background cron job to periodically sweep stale deps — adds infrastructure for a rare scenario, delays cleanup until next sweep
Explicit 'Clean dependencies' button — adds UI surface, requires user awareness of stale deps
factory_decisions.rationale: The stages/[id] RSC already loads tasks and deps. Running removeStaleDependencies on the loaded edges + deleting stale rows from the DB is zero-latency (same query, no extra round trip for the common case of zero stale deps). Matches AC-DV-2's 'when loaded' trigger literally. ON DELETE CASCADE on task_dependencies already handles most cases, so stale deps are rare — this is a safety net.

factory_decisions.decision: Available dep targets include ALL project tasks (not just other-stage tasks)
factory_decisions.context: The original stages/[id] page filtered availableTasks to exclude this stage's tasks. But dependencies within the same stage are valid (e.g., 'Pour foundation' before 'Damp proof course').
alternatives_considered: Keep the cross-stage-only filter — prevents same-stage deps, which are valid and common in construction sequences
Show all tasks including self — the modal filter already excludes the task being edited
factory_decisions.rationale: Changed availableTasks to include all project tasks. The dep editor modal filters out the task itself (opt.id !== depEditTarget.id). Same-stage deps are the most common case in construction (sequential tasks within a build phase).

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for daily-briefing-view story
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 1 BUILD (lib/briefing/operations.ts + tests + RefreshButton), 1 EXTEND (app/(app)/briefing/page.tsx — already had Upcoming Orders section, extended with Today Tasks and Shift Alerts sections). Reused: selectUpcomingOrders + materialDeadlineStatus from lib/materials/operations.ts, MaterialDeadlineBadge component, createClient from lib/supabase/server.ts, AppShell layout from components/layout/AppShell.tsx, warm earth-tone design tokens from globals.css.

factory_decisions.decision: Parallel DB fetches via Promise.all for briefing page performance
factory_decisions.context: AC-DB-6 requires FCP < 1.5s on 4G. The briefing page needs tasks, materials, and shift_alerts. Three sequential Supabase queries would triple the waterfall.
alternatives_considered: Sequential queries — simpler code but 3x latency on the critical path
Single RPC/view joining all three — requires a new migration + stored procedure for a read-only page
Client-side SWR with loading skeletons — adds client JS, worse FCP, defeats server component benefits
factory_decisions.rationale: Promise.all([taskRes, matRes, alertRes]) executes all three queries concurrently. Server component means zero client JS for the data rendering. Combined with Vercel edge caching of static assets, this keeps FCP well under the 1.5s target.

factory_decisions.decision: Shift Alerts section renders from shift_alerts table (empty until populated by cascade)
factory_decisions.context: AC-DB-1 requires a Shift Alerts section. The shift_alerts table exists in the schema but cascade_task_dates() does not yet write to it (documented factory_question from foundation cycle).
alternatives_considered: Skip the section entirely — violates AC-DB-1 three-section requirement
Compute alerts client-side by diffing task dates — unreliable without a baseline, adds client JS
Write a migration to add a trigger on task date updates — expands scope beyond this story
factory_decisions.rationale: Wiring the UI to the real shift_alerts table now means the section will auto-populate when the shift-alerts story adds the cascade writer. Empty state is graceful. No throwaway code.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for task-quick-actions story
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 1 BUILD (toggleTaskComplete server action + computeToggleResult pure fn + BriefingTaskList client component), 2 EXTEND (selectTodayTasks to include complete tasks, briefing page to use client component), 3 REUSE (logTaskDelay action, RefreshButton useTransition+router.refresh pattern, warm earth-tone design tokens). Key reuse: existing router.refresh() pattern from RefreshButton for AC-QA-4 in-place updates.

factory_decisions.decision: selectTodayTasks includes complete tasks for undo support
factory_decisions.context: AC-QA-2 requires undoing completion from the briefing. If complete tasks were filtered out (original AC-DB-2 behavior), toggling a task would make it disappear immediately.
alternatives_considered: Keep original filter (exclude complete) and re-fetch on toggle — task disappears during the round trip, confusing UX
Client-side local filter override — diverges from server truth, breaks on page refresh
Include complete tasks sorted after incomplete (chosen) — task stays visible with strikethrough, user can undo
factory_decisions.rationale: Including complete tasks with a sort bias (incomplete first) preserves the undo flow while keeping active work prominent. The empty-state message still shows correctly when ALL tasks are out of range. This is a minor behavioral change logged as a divergence for the evaluator.

factory_decisions.decision: Quick actions link to /tasks/:id with hash anchors rather than inline modals
factory_decisions.context: AC-QA-3 specifies Log Delay, Add Note, View Detail as quick actions. Log Delay already exists on /tasks/:id as TaskDelayLogger. Add Note needs a notes-editing UI that does not yet exist.
alternatives_considered: Inline modal per quick action on /briefing — duplicates TaskDelayLogger and creates a new notes editor; bloats briefing client JS
Navigate to /tasks/:id (chosen) — reuses existing per-task controls, keeps briefing lightweight
Bottom sheet on mobile — custom animation + portal complexity for what is a simple navigation
factory_decisions.rationale: Link to /tasks/:id (with #delay and #notes hash anchors) reuses the existing delay logger on the detail page and avoids building a duplicate. The Add Note action links to #notes which will activate when a notes-editing section is added to the task detail in a future story. View Detail is a plain navigation. Keeps the briefing page focused on its primary role (status overview) rather than becoming a full task-editing surface.

factory_decisions.decision: Swipe gesture uses 60px threshold with native touchStart/touchEnd tracking
factory_decisions.context: AC-QA-3 requires swipe left on mobile to reveal quick actions. Options: CSS-only with scroll-snap, JS touch tracking, or a gesture library.
alternatives_considered: Install a gesture library (react-swipeable, use-gesture) — adds a dependency for one component
CSS scroll-snap with overflow-x — limited control over snap points, hard to wire dismiss behavior
Native touch tracking with 60px threshold (chosen) — zero dependencies, full control over swipe/dismiss
factory_decisions.rationale: 60px threshold is comfortable for intentional swipes on mobile (avoids false positives from scroll jitter). touchStartX ref tracks per-task independently so multiple rows can be swiped. Swiping right or tapping outside dismisses. CSS translateX transition provides smooth slide animation. No third-party dependency for a single interaction pattern.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for upcoming-orders-view story
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 1 BUILD (UpcomingOrderCards component + daysUntilOrderBy + formatMaterialCost), 1 EXTEND (briefing page — added card rendering + extra material fields to query). Reused: selectUpcomingOrders + materialDeadlineStatus from lib/materials/operations.ts, MaterialDeadlineBadge component, updateMaterialStatus server action from app/actions/materials.ts, existing material query pattern from briefing page.

factory_decisions.decision: Accordion pattern (one expanded card at a time) for order detail expansion
factory_decisions.context: AC-UO-3 says tap to expand with inline detail. Multiple cards could be expanded simultaneously or exclusively.
alternatives_considered: Allow multiple cards expanded at once — clutters the horizontal scroll on mobile, harder to scan
Modal overlay on tap — heavier UI, blocks the rest of the briefing, overkill for 4-5 fields
Navigate to /materials/:id detail page — extra navigation, breaks the briefing-as-dashboard flow
factory_decisions.rationale: Single expanded card at a time (accordion) keeps the horizontal scroll tight on mobile and focuses attention on one order. Expanding a different card auto-collapses the previous one. State is a single expandedId string, minimal React re-renders.

factory_decisions.decision: Section hidden entirely when empty (AC-UO-4) — no empty state message
factory_decisions.context: AC-UO-4 explicitly says section is hidden (not shown with empty state) to keep briefing focused. Previous implementation showed "No orders due in the next 7 days." message.
alternatives_considered: Keep the empty state message — contradicts AC-UO-4 literally
Collapse header but keep a thin placeholder — still visible, still clutters
factory_decisions.rationale: Conditional rendering: {upcomingCards.length > 0 && <section>...}. When no materials due in 7 days, the section is entirely absent from the DOM. Briefing flows directly from Today Tasks to Shift Alerts. Matches the spec requirement to keep the briefing focused on actionable information.

factory_decisions.decision: Shift alerts record planned_end (not planned_start) as old_value/new_value for task movements
factory_decisions.context: AC-SA-1 says show "[Task] moved from [old date] to [new date]". Could use start or end dates.
alternatives_considered: planned_start — shows when work begins, but a duration change moves the end without moving the start
Both start and end as a range — more info but clutters the alert text
planned_end — shows when the task finishes, which is what downstream deps and the user care about
factory_decisions.rationale: planned_end is what cascade_task_dates() propagates and what determines downstream impact. A 3-day delay on task A means task B (which depends on A) starts 3 days later — the relevant signal is A's new finish date. Matches the formatShiftAlert template which shows a single date pair.

factory_decisions.decision: Shift alert inserts are best-effort — failure does not block the cascade result
factory_decisions.context: insertShiftAlerts runs after the cascade RPC succeeds. If the insert fails (e.g. RLS issue, network), the cascade already committed.
alternatives_considered: Wrap cascade + alert insert in a transaction — requires a stored procedure or RPC, adds migration complexity for a non-critical write
Fail the whole action if alerts fail — makes a UX notification more important than the actual data update
Best-effort insert, ignore errors — cascade is the authoritative mutation; alerts are informational
factory_decisions.rationale: The cascade is the source of truth for dates. Shift alerts are a convenience display. Failing to record an alert is preferable to failing to cascade a date change. The briefing page still renders correctly with or without alerts.

factory_decisions.decision: Builder-defined acceptance criteria for push-notifications (spec was empty)
factory_decisions.context: state.json story entry for push-notifications has no acceptance_criteria field — only name, affected_screens (/settings), depends_on (daily-briefing-view). story_context.spec was just {id, name}.
alternatives_considered: Block the story with a factory_question asking a human to write ACs — defeats autonomous loop
Skip the story entirely — push notifications are in-scope per vision.json
factory_decisions.rationale: Defined AC-PN-1 (SW registration on app load), AC-PN-2 (enable → permission prompt → subscribe → persist), AC-PN-3 (disable → unsubscribe → mark inactive), AC-PN-4 (blocked state with browser settings hint), AC-PN-5 (upsert notification_preferences on first /settings visit). These map directly to the existing foundation infrastructure (sw.js, /api/push/subscribe, push_subscriptions table, notification_preferences table) and the affected screen (/settings).

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for push-notifications story
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 4 BUILD (lib/notifications/push.ts pure helpers, ServiceWorkerRegistrar, PushNotificationToggle, ensureNotificationPreferences server action), 1 EXTEND (app/(app)/settings/page.tsx — placeholder replaced with real notification sections), 3 REUSE (app/api/push/subscribe/route.ts, public/sw.js push handler, app/api/cron/daily-check/route.ts).

factory_decisions.decision: ServiceWorkerRegistrar placed in (app) layout, not root layout
factory_decisions.context: SW registration needs to happen once on app load. Could go in root layout (covers auth pages too) or (app) layout (only authenticated pages).
alternatives_considered: Root layout — registers SW on login/signup pages too, wasting a registration call before the user is authenticated
Individual pages — duplicates the registration call and risks missing it on new pages
factory_decisions.rationale: (app) layout is the authenticated shell. All push-notification features require an authenticated user. Registering the SW here means it only activates after login, and it runs once for the entire app session via useEffect([]).

factory_decisions.decision: ensureNotificationPreferences upserts on /settings page load (not on signup)
factory_decisions.context: Foundation eval found that cron INNER JOIN on notification_preferences silently excludes users without a prefs row. Options: insert on signup, insert on first /settings visit, or change the JOIN to LEFT JOIN.
alternatives_considered: Insert in signup action — requires modifying auth flow, risk of partial signup if insert fails
Change cron to LEFT JOIN with defaults — loses the ability to store per-user overrides
Insert on first /settings visit (chosen) — lazy creation, zero auth flow changes
factory_decisions.rationale: Lazy upsert on /settings is the simplest fix: one server action call, race-condition safe (read-then-insert with conflict retry). Users who never visit /settings still get excluded from cron notifications — which is acceptable since they never expressed interest in configuring notifications. The notification-settings story (next in the milestone) will add toggle editing.

factory_decisions.decision: Builder-defined acceptance criteria for notification-settings (spec was empty)
factory_decisions.context: state.json story entry for notification-settings has no acceptance_criteria — only id, name, affected_screens (/settings), depends_on (push-notifications). story_context.spec was just {id, name}.
alternatives_considered: Block the story asking for human-written ACs — defeats autonomous loop; the existing /settings page already shows the three boolean prefs + warning days as read-only, making the needed interaction clear
Treat as already done since prefs display on /settings — ignores that they are static text not interactive controls
Add only toggles without the warning-days stepper — leaves order_warning_days uneditable despite being a user-facing config
factory_decisions.rationale: Defined AC-NS-1 (toggle switches for booleans), AC-NS-2 (stepper for warning days 1-14), AC-NS-3 (optimistic UI with rollback), AC-NS-4 (validation matching DB CHECK). These four criteria map directly to the story name (Notification Preferences) and the /settings screen where the read-only labels already existed from push-notifications story. Each AC has tests and concrete server action calls.

factory_decisions.decision: Extracted BriefingHeader as a separate component rather than adding inline JSX
factory_decisions.context: AC-PI-1/2/3 required adding project name to /briefing header. The inline header JSX was simple enough to modify directly, but extracting a component enables testing with @testing-library/react (server component page.tsx cannot be rendered in vitest).
alternatives_considered: Inline JSX change only — untestable in vitest since page.tsx is an async RSC
Pure helper function for header text — over-engineering for a one-line text addition
factory_decisions.rationale: BriefingHeader component encapsulates the header layout (h1 + subtitle + RefreshButton), is testable via @testing-library/react, and keeps page.tsx focused on data fetching. The component is also reusable if the header pattern appears on other pages.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for gantt-chart-render
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 1 BUILD (GanttChart component + pure computation), 1 EXTEND (/schedule page.tsx to add Gantt rendering + task/dep fetching), 0 REUSE, 0 REFACTOR-THEN-BUILD. Key reuse: existing StageManager stayed in place, types/database.ts Task and TaskDependency types informed the GanttTask shape, stage color palette from StageManager reused in Gantt bars.

factory_decisions.decision: Pure computation + client component architecture for Gantt chart (no Canvas/SVG-only approach)
factory_decisions.context: AC-GR-1 requires an interactive Gantt chart. Options: full SVG/Canvas rendering, HTML div-based bars with SVG overlay for arrows, or a third-party Gantt library.
alternatives_considered: Third-party Gantt library (e.g. frappe-gantt, dhtmlx-gantt) — heavy dependencies, hard to style to match the warm earth-tone design, McKinley says no for non-core infra
Full SVG rendering — more control but harder to add interactive features later (tooltips, click handlers, accessibility), slower to implement
Full Canvas rendering — best performance for 1000+ tasks but no DOM accessibility, overkill for typical 20-50 task projects
factory_decisions.rationale: HTML divs for bars (positioned absolutely, styled with Tailwind) + SVG overlay for dependency arrows. Bars are real DOM elements with title attributes, testable via data-testid, and extensible for click handlers in the gantt-inline-edit story. Pure computation functions in lib/gantt/compute.ts (date range, bar positioning, arrow coordinates) are fully unit-testable. SVG arrows use a simple right-angle path with arrowhead markers.

factory_decisions.decision: Inclusive-day bar width: widthDays = duration_days (matching existing inclusive-day semantics)
factory_decisions.context: AC-GR-2 says 'width = duration_days'. The project uses inclusive-day semantics (planned_end = planned_start + duration - 1). A 5-day task starting Apr 1 ends Apr 5 and the bar should span 5 day columns.
alternatives_considered: Exclusive: width = planned_end - planned_start → 4 columns for a 5-day task — misleading visual
Width from dates: daysBetween(planned_start, planned_end) + 1 — correct but redundant since duration_days already encodes this
factory_decisions.rationale: Using duration_days directly as widthDays is the simplest correct approach, consistent with how tasks were inserted (planned_end = planned_start + duration_days - 1). The Gantt bar visually represents the actual working days.

factory_decisions.decision: Mobile Gantt shows stages-only accordion (not a scaled-down full Gantt)
factory_decisions.context: AC-GR-6 specifies 'stages-only view shown by default with horizontal scroll; tapping a stage expands to show its tasks'. Two interpretations: a miniaturized full Gantt with pinch-zoom, or a list of stages that expand to show task bars.
alternatives_considered: Miniaturized full Gantt with pinch-zoom — complex touch handling, bars become unreadable at phone widths
Table/list view with no bars — simpler but loses the visual timeline which is the point of a Gantt
factory_decisions.rationale: Accordion with per-stage horizontal-scrollable task bars matches the spec literally (stages-only default, tap to expand) and is usable on phone screens. Each expanded stage shows its task bars within a scrollable container at the same DAY_WIDTH as desktop, so the visual language is consistent. Dependency arrows are omitted on mobile for clarity — they add visual noise on small screens and the gantt-inline-edit story is desktop-only anyway.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for gantt-zoom-navigate
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 1 BUILD (zoom toolbar + config), 2 REUSE (TodayMarker for AC-GZ-2, overflow-x-auto for AC-GZ-3), 1 EXTEND (DateHeader to support weekly/monthly modes). Key reuse: existing TodayMarker component served AC-GZ-2 without modification. Existing overflow-x-auto CSS served AC-GZ-3 without modification. computeTodayOffset shares daysBetween from existing compute.ts.

factory_decisions.decision: Zoom dayWidth values: week=28, month=8, full=3
factory_decisions.context: AC-GZ-1 requires three zoom levels. Need to choose pixel-per-day scaling that keeps the chart usable at each level.
alternatives_considered: Logarithmic scaling — complex, harder to reason about bar positioning
Larger month/full values (e.g. 14, 6) — still too wide for year-long projects
Smaller full value (1px) — bars become invisible at low task counts
factory_decisions.rationale: Week=28 matches original DAY_WIDTH (proven readable for daily labels). Month=8 gives a 365-day project ~2920px width (fits in ~2 scroll lengths on 1440px screen). Full=3 gives a 365-day project ~1095px (fits on screen without scroll). Labels auto-hide below 30px span width to prevent overflow.

factory_decisions.decision: InteractiveTaskBar uses document-level mousemove/mouseup listeners for drag tracking
factory_decisions.context: AC-GE-1/AC-GE-2 require drag interactions on Gantt bars. Options: HTML5 drag-and-drop, pointer events with setPointerCapture, or manual mousemove/mouseup on document.
alternatives_considered: HTML5 drag-and-drop: designed for drag-to-target, not pixel-precise resize/move. dragImage snap is jarring.
Pointer capture: cleaner API but less browser support and no natural click-vs-drag threshold.
dnd-kit library: full library for a two-interaction feature; boring tech principle says no new deps for simple drags
factory_decisions.rationale: Document-level mousemove/mouseup with a 4px threshold to distinguish click from drag. Gives pixel-precise preview updates via React state, works on all desktop browsers, zero new dependencies.

factory_decisions.decision: Dependency constraint validation runs client-side before server action (AC-GE-4)
factory_decisions.context: AC-GE-4 requires snapping back to original position on invalid drop. Options: validate client-side and block the server call, or call the server and rollback on error.
alternatives_considered: Server-side validation only: requires a round trip, bar visually jumps then snaps back, feels janky
Both client and server: validate client-side for instant feedback, server validates again as safety net
factory_decisions.rationale: Client-side check via validateTaskDrop (pure function) gives instant snap-back with no server round trip. The server action still goes through verifyProjectOwnership and cascade_task_dates which would fail on genuine constraint violations. Dependency data is fresh from the server render.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for gantt-inline-edit
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 2 BUILD (lib/gantt/drag.ts, TaskDetailPanel.tsx), 3 EXTEND (GanttChart.tsx, tasks.ts actions, schedule/page.tsx, globals.css). Key reuse: existing updateTaskDuration for AC-GE-1 resize, existing cascade_task_dates RPC for both resize and move, existing computeTaskBars/computeDateRange from lib/gantt/compute.ts.

factory_decisions.decision: Builder-defined acceptance criteria for photo-storage (spec was empty)
factory_decisions.context: state.json story entry for photo-storage has no acceptance_criteria field — only name, affected_entities (Photo), affected_screens (/tasks/:id, /stages/:id, /photos). story_context.spec was just {id, name}.
alternatives_considered: Block the story with a factory_question asking a human to write ACs — defeats autonomous loop
Skip the story as already covered by storage bucket setup — ignores the UI and upload flow
factory_decisions.rationale: Defined AC-PS-1 (upload with validation), AC-PS-2 (thumbnails on task detail), AC-PS-3 (gallery grouped by stage), AC-PS-4 (delete removes storage + DB row), AC-PS-5 (client-side file validation). These map directly to the story name and affected screens.

factory_decisions.decision: Search Before Building audit for photo-storage
factory_decisions.context: Pre-implementation code reuse analysis
factory_decisions.rationale: Found: 2 BUILD (lib/photos/operations.ts pure fns, components/photos/TaskPhotosManager.tsx), 2 EXTEND (/tasks/:id page to add photo query + component, /photos page to replace skeleton placeholder), 1 REUSE (existing Photo type from types/database.ts, photos table schema, storage bucket config). Key reuse: Supabase Storage photos bucket already configured with RLS, Photo interface from types/database.ts informed the server action types, createClient/LooseClient pattern from other actions.

factory_decisions.decision: Signed URLs with 60s expiry for photo previews (not public bucket)
factory_decisions.context: Photos bucket is private (set during foundation). Options: make bucket public, use signed URLs, or use a proxy route.
